FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up (2019–2022), 10,048 adolescents, ages 12–13 years, 21 geographically diverse sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using multiple linear regression to examine associations between media parenting practices and adolescent screen use outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Pediatric Research, 2025; not identified as preprint or duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Parenting practices (screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, monitoring, limiting) are the independent variables. Outcomes are adolescent screen time hours and problematic screen use scores. The IV is parenting/family context (not individual-trait-level), and the DV is screen behavior, not an individual-trait outcome. No pairing of smartphone/social media variable with individual-trait-level outcome exists.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because the research question examines parenting practices (contextual/behavioral) as IV predicting adolescent screen time/problematic use as outcome. Neither side of the relationship includes a qualifying smartphone/SM variable paired with an individual-trait-level variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states review of Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data covering over 11,000 participants, ages 9–15 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Explicitly a review article. PURPOSE OF REVIEW: To review recent literature based on Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data. Not an original empirical analysis.

notes: Paper is a narrative review of ABCD Study findings, not original empirical analysis. Exclusion code FT-EC3.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study data from period 2 follow-up (2018–2020) to period 3 follow-up (2019–2021), 9,095 adolescents, mean age 12.0±0.7 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis with multivariable linear regression models estimating associations between cyberbullying victimization and manic symptoms 1 year later

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Acta Paediatrica, 2025; not identified as preprint or duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the independent variable; manic symptoms is the outcome. Per criteria: "Cyberbullying alone (with no separately-measured SM/phone variable) does NOT qualify." No qualifying smartphone or social media use variable is measured separately from cyberbullying. Cyberbullying is not operationalized as SM or phone use.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because cyberbullying alone, without a separately-measured smartphone or social media variable, does not meet the qualifying exposure criterion. Exclusion code FT-EC2.
